one_line_takeaway: A survey of recommender-system robustness (adversarial shilling attacks/defenses plus non-adversarial data issues) whose concrete contribution is ShillingREC, an open-source library that fairly benchmarks poisoning attacks on both explicit and implicit feedback, showing sparse datasets and supervised models are most vulnerable while learned attacks (AUSH/RevAdv) beat heuristics.
key_finding: Three empirical takeaways from the ShillingREC benchmark: (1) Shilling attacks are more effective on smaller/sparser datasets - attackers achieve higher Prediction Shift on ML-100K than on the larger ML-1M, because sparse interactions let biased fake-user signals dominate. (2) In implicit-feedback settings, models trained with unsupervised/self-supervised signals are more robust: SGL (contrastive learning) consistently beats LightGCN on the target-item robustness metrics (T-NDCG, T-HR) under AUSH attack, attributed to the filtering/consistency effect of contrastive learning. (3) Learned attacks (optimization-based RevAdv, GAN-based AUSH) generally outperform fixed-rule heuristics (Random, Bandwagon) - e.g. on LastFM, AUSH gives the strongest manipulation across all recommenders - because they generate more realistic, effective fake behavior. Methodologically, ShillingREC's contribution is providing a single framework where explicit and implicit attacks are each evaluated fairly in their native data regime.
core_contribution: This is a hybrid survey-plus-artifact paper on the robustness of recommender systems. Its OWN contributions (as distinct from the large body of prior work it reviews) are four: (1) a taxonomy that splits recommender-system robustness into 'adversarial robustness' (defense against intentionally injected malicious data, i.e. shilling / profile-injection / poisoning attacks) and 'non-adversarial robustness' (degradation from data sparsity, natural noise in implicit feedback, and data imbalance); (2) a compiled catalogue of commonly-used datasets and evaluation/robustness metrics for this task; (3) ShillingREC, an open-source PyTorch evaluation library (released at github.com/chengleileilei/ShillingREC) that, unlike prior benchmarks RecAd (rating-only) and ARLib (which forces rating attacks to be re-adapted to implicit data), supports poisoning attacks on BOTH explicit-rating and implicit-feedback data natively so each attack class is evaluated in its area of strength; and (4) original benchmark experiments run through ShillingREC. NOTE: the many concrete attack methods discussed at length (Random/Average/Bandwagon/Love-Hate/Segment heuristics, PGA/SGLD/SRWA/TNA/DL-Attack/AIA/RAPU/Infmix optimization attacks, DCGAN/AUSH/RecUp/GOAT/TrialAttack/Leg-UP GAN attacks, LOKI/PoisonRec/CopyAttack RL attacks) and the defense methods (SVM-TIA, CNN-SAD, PCA-VarSelect, HySAD, LTSMF, etc.) are all CITED prior work surveyed by the authors, NOT novel methods introduced by this paper.
threat_model: Shilling / profile-injection (data-poisoning) attacks against collaborative-filtering recommenders. Because CF relies on collective user signals and cannot natively distinguish genuine from fake users, an attacker injects fabricated user profiles (fake users with crafted ratings or interactions) to manipulate recommendations. Attacks are characterized by three parameters: attack size (number of injected fake users), target item(s), and filler items (other interactions added to disguise the fake profiles). Goals are targeted 'push' (inflate a target item's exposure/rating), targeted 'nuke' (suppress an item), or non-targeted (degrade overall system quality). The survey organizes attacker knowledge into white-box (model structure + parameters known), gray-box (algorithm known, parameters unknown, often via a local surrogate/proxy model), and black-box (neither known, e.g. RL-based attacks). The paper's own experiments simulate a compromised/poisoned recommender by injecting fake users and measuring recommendation degradation.
limitations: (1) As a survey, the bulk of the paper is a description of others' methods; the genuinely novel technical artifact is the ShillingREC library and its benchmark, which is relatively modest (a handful of classic attacks and recommenders; no evaluation of the many advanced attacks it catalogues, e.g. TrialAttack, Leg-UP, LOKI, RAPU, Infmix, nor of most detection/defense methods it surveys). (2) The experiments evaluate recommender robustness under attack but do not benchmark the detection or robust-algorithm defenses that occupy Section IV. (3) The authors themselves flag (Sec IX) that all evaluation relies on artificially simulated fake users - no real-world fraud data - so simulated user-item-rating triplets cannot capture real attacker feature complexity, limiting external validity. (4) Robustness conclusions rest on a single fixed attack configuration (20% attacker size, 0.5% targets, K=50); sensitivity to these parameters is not swept. (5) PS and T-metrics are not normalized across datasets/models, making cross-setting magnitude comparisons only loosely interpretable.

quantitative_results: Explicit-feedback Prediction Shift (PS), higher = weaker robustness. On ML-100K under Random Attack: SGL PS=2.0922, NeuMF PS=1.9365, LightGCN PS=1.8517, MF PS=1.6996 (all with no-attack baseline PS=0).
Clean (no-attack) accuracy on ML-100K: MF RMSE=1.1778/MAE=0.9823; NeuMF RMSE=0.9556/MAE=0.7520; LightGCN RMSE=0.9773/MAE=0.7678; SGL RMSE=0.9974/MAE=0.7820.
Dataset-size effect: attack PS values are systematically higher on ML-100K than on the larger ML-1M (e.g. LightGCN Random-Attack PS drops from 1.8517 on ML-100K to 1.3987 on ML-1M).
Attack config held constant: attacker size = 20% of training users; #target items = 0.5% of attacker size (unpopular items); #filler items = average ratings per user; Top-K evaluated at K=50.
Qualitative benchmark findings: SGL > LightGCN on T-NDCG and T-HR under AUSH across all implicit datasets; AUSH gives strongest attack across all models on LastFM.
baselines_compared: Recommendation models: MF, NeuMF, LightGCN, SGL (explicit track); plus CDAE and SGDL (implicit track)
Attack models (explicit): Random Attack, Average Attack, Love/Hate Attack (heuristic), AUSH (GAN-based)
Attack models (implicit): Random Attack, Bandwagon Attack (heuristic), AUSH (GAN-based), RevAdv (optimization-based)
Competing libraries contrasted (not run as baselines but positioned against): RecAd (rating-only), ARLib (implicit-only, force-adapts rating attacks); ShillingREC supports both natively
